item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i still love how absolutely crazy this is, which fits the poem its based on.
I am an irreparable fan boy of the Jabberwock; both poem and beast. Don't know how I managed to miss upvoting this. And now I claim +50. O frabjous day!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was sorry to see this had gone away, long ago. Glad to see it's back, even if it only "works" for 10% of the population now. And yeah, that factor and the aversion to it after the fact help detract from the "fightan good" problem, even though I felt that wasn't a real problem for it before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. I hadn't even thought of that. I didn't see the reason for the Foundation to bother recording it, as it doesn't particularly affect the item itself in any way, and the documentation appears to imply the inscription is little more than a joke on the part of this 'Richard' fellow. I'll change that if it bothers you.

EDIT: And done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, look. I know this is based on "Jabberwocky". You know this is based on "Jabberwocky". Everyone here knows this is based on "Jabberwocky".

But someone working for the Foundation and newly assigned to the item? They might not know. So it should say, in the description, that the lines of poetry are taken from "Jabberwocky".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like things with anomalies that mess with your head. Upvoted.

I think you should add some tests on the purple liquid or otherwise give the liquid more of a reason to be in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not once does the blade go "snicker-snack", and this seems a shame.
Maybe the instantaneous stroke could be accompanied by a distinctive sound caused by displaced air?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, you're right. I didn't realise that I'd implied probabilities, they were supposed to be statements of the percentages that had been observed, where someone doing the admin could just do the conversions. I'd re-do the whole paragraph, but I suspect that would probably be wrong as well, so they've all been dropped back to 1 significant figure.

Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About 200,000.

Rioghail, no actual scientist or statistician would quote three significant figures after only 23 tests. Even two significant figures would have substantial error bars. Actually, with a sample that small, you'd probably just quote the raw numbers and not try to estimate probabilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You successfully hit a large number of my Lewis Carroll poetry fancatapult buttons in the first paragraph that I actually stopped to upvote then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have an issue with the .?% dealies. Do you have any idea how many people we'd have to test it on to get that accurate of a percentage?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little weighty in the writing, but I really can't not upvote something based on Alice in Wonderland. This sentence however…

This body plan appears to have at least seven limbs, three tail-like appendages, no head or analogous appendage, and an asymmetric body plan.

Yo dawg, I heard you like body plans…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dictionary.reference.com says otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Double post. Ignore this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did remove 'approximately' because going over 3 significant figures is definitely over-specification.

I'm not changing the percentages themselves, though. In a scientific sense, 3 significant figures. (i.e. 12.3) is not extremely specific. They also don't indicate that the Foundation has tested them on thousands of people (they are calculated from an actual number of tests - 23 in total). They might not add anything to the article, but I don't see what's wrong with them letting them stay either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've changed it to 'orient', because this is written for Drewbear and so what he says goes. But the two words have the same meaning according to the Oxford English Dictionary, and 'orientate' is the preferred term in the UK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, 'Approximately' followed by a percentage with a decimal point does not jibe. This is a scenario where getting down to decimal point level of accuracy removes all need to say 'approximately'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're not the same word, however. "Orientate" is an intransitive verb (does not need an object) meaning "turning to the east (orient)". "Orient" is a transitive verb (requires an object) meaning "to find or give direction". "Orientate" itself is a back-formation from the word "orientation".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You notice that you can't, say, swing a stick as fast under water as you can through the air? Now imagine that the stick behaves that way, even though you're not submerged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The extremely specific percentages don't add anything to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's kind of cool, but I second Drewbear about some confusing sentences. For instance

Additionally, SCP-1871 will occasionally begin to behave as if it is submerged in a highly viscous liquid when it is not, and will maintain this anomalous state for several hours at a time before reverting to normal. Only SCP-1871 is affected by these anomalous properties.

How does a sword behaves when submerged in a liquid vs not? After reading that part a few times I suppose you mean the wielder will feel like they are moving it through a thick liquid, although their own bodies and limbs feel normal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The vorpal sword, huh? Turned out surprisingly well. I just have some nitpicks- how does the purple residue indicate that it was used in combat (as opposed to simply cutting something), and the reference to the direction the pommel is pointing is sort of unclear, as Drewbear mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I like this. I like this a lot.

Here, I am upvote 4 yu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"orient", not "orientate."

They're both words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I like this, but there are a few phrasings that I find a bit confusing:

If placed on a flat surface, SCP-1871 will orientate itself so that it is balanced on the hilt with the sword point facing directly upwards, although the sword is not weighted

So it stands up on its hilt/pommel? Also, I think you meant "orient", not "orientate."

causing their sense of balance to shift so that they perceive gravity as act parallel to the blade of the sword, acting towards the pommel of the sword

Okay, so their balance has been fucked, but I'm not getting a good grip on exactly how they're perceiving gravity. Do you mean that they feel like the pommel of the sword is "down" and the point of the sword is "up"?

I really like how a not-insignificant percentage get the Jabberwock bodymapped onto them.

In my opinion, all challenges met and well. And thank you for the gift. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A vorpal sword. Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. This is a good way to write mysterious SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, dude. I enjoyed this one a fair amount, and therefore have upvoted it. Only major thing that bothered me is having two paragraphs in a row start with "A further <x>%". I've been told that I'm the only one bothered by this, but it still bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What did I just read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*reads*

/confused

*rereads*

Ah, what the heck. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For Drewbear as a Birthday present. I only saw your challenge tonight so I cranked this thing out as quickly as possible.

This thing completes four challenges at once (though only two intentionally): Bright's 24-hour challenge, Drewbear's non-sight/hearing sensory effect challenge, Bright's 'weapon unrelated to fightan good' challenge, and Djoric's challenge to update a pre-Mass Edit article. This one is based on the (rewritten)version of SCP-267 - Vorpal Sword, the author of which I sadly do not know and so cannot credit.
